facts.title: Hair Growth Happens in Cycles
facts.description: Hair grows in three phases: Anagen (growth), Catagen (transition), and Telogen (resting). At any time, 85–90% of your hair is in the growing phase.
facts.fullDetail: The anagen phase lasts 2–6 years, determining your maximum hair length. The catagen phase is a brief 2–3 week transition where the follicle shrinks. The telogen phase lasts about 3 months before the old hair sheds and a new anagen cycle begins. Disruptions to this cycle — from stress, nutrition, or hormones — are the root cause of most hair loss conditions.

facts.title: Hair is the Second Fastest-Growing Tissue
facts.description: After bone marrow, hair is the second fastest growing tissue in the human body, growing approximately 1.25 cm per month.
facts.fullDetail: Hair grows at roughly 0.5 inches (1.25 cm) per month — about 6 inches per year. This rate is influenced by age (faster in your 20s–30s), season (slightly faster in summer), genetics, and overall health. Hormonal imbalances, nutritional deficiencies, and chronic illness can all slow this rate significantly.

facts.title: Protein is the Building Block of Hair
facts.description: Hair is made of keratin — a fibrous structural protein. Adequate protein intake is essential for healthy hair growth.
facts.fullDetail: Each strand of hair is approximately 95% keratin protein. When dietary protein is insufficient, the body prioritises vital organs and diverts amino acids away from hair production. This leads to diffuse shedding (telogen effluvium) within 2–3 months of protein deficiency. Aim for 0.8–1.2g of protein per kg of body weight daily for optimal hair support.

facts.title: Your Scalp Has ~100,000 Hair Follicles
facts.description: The average scalp contains 100,000–150,000 hair follicles. Follicle count is fixed at birth and cannot increase.
facts.fullDetail: Follicle density varies by hair colour: blondes average ~150,000, brunettes ~110,000, and redheads ~90,000. You shed 50–100 hairs daily as part of the normal telogen cycle — this is not hair loss. Once a follicle is permanently destroyed (by scarring alopecia or severe traction), it cannot regenerate. This is why early intervention is critical.

facts.title: Sebum is Your Scalp's Natural Conditioner
facts.description: Sebaceous glands attached to each follicle produce sebum — an oily substance that moisturises hair and scalp, protecting against breakage.
facts.fullDetail: Sebum production is regulated by androgens (especially testosterone and DHT). Overproduction leads to greasy hair and may worsen conditions like seborrhoeic dermatitis. Underproduction leads to dry, brittle hair. Sebum also has mild antimicrobial properties, protecting the scalp microbiome. Washing frequency should be tailored to your sebum production rate.

facts.title: Stress Triggers Telogen Effluvium
facts.description: Significant physical or emotional stress can push large numbers of hair follicles into the resting (telogen) phase, causing diffuse shedding 2–3 months later.
facts.fullDetail: Telogen effluvium from stress is typically temporary and reversible once the stressor resolves. Common triggers include major illness, surgery, childbirth, crash dieting, or severe emotional trauma. The shedding appears alarming — sometimes 300+ hairs per day — but full recovery usually occurs within 6–9 months. Chronic stress, however, can sustain the shedding cycle.

facts.title: Exercise Boosts Scalp Circulation
facts.description: Regular aerobic exercise improves blood flow throughout the body, including the scalp, delivering more oxygen and nutrients to hair follicles.
facts.fullDetail: Exercise increases cardiac output and dilates capillaries. Studies show regular exercisers have improved scalp microcirculation compared to sedentary individuals. This supports the anagen phase and can reduce the impact of DHT on susceptible follicles. Even 30 minutes of moderate cardio 3–4 times weekly can make a meaningful difference to scalp health.

facts.title: Myth: Shaving Makes Hair Grow Back Thicker
facts.description: Shaved hair appears coarser as it grows back because the blunt cut lacks the tapered tip, not because the hair is actually thicker.

facts.title: Myth: Daily Shampooing Damages Hair

facts.title: Each Hair Follicle Works Independently
facts.description: Every hair follicle on your scalp cycles through growth and shedding independently, so you don't lose all your hair at once.
facts.fullDetail: If all follicles were synchronised, humans would go completely bald temporarily each cycle — as some animals do seasonally. The asynchronous cycling of ~100,000 follicles ensures constant hair coverage. This independence is why targeted treatments can work follicle-by-follicle and why hair loss is usually gradual.

facts.title: Hair Colour Comes From Melanin
facts.description: Hair colour is determined by melanocytes in the follicle that produce melanin — eumelanin for dark shades and pheomelanin for red/blonde tones.
facts.fullDetail: Melanocytes are pigment-producing cells located in the hair bulb. They inject melanin granules into the growing hair shaft during the anagen phase. As we age, these cells gradually reduce melanin production, leading to grey and eventually white hair. Hydrogen peroxide produced naturally in hair cells also bleaches hair from within over time.

facts.title: Shedding 50–100 Hairs Per Day is Normal

facts.fullDetail: On average, 10–15% of scalp follicles are in the telogen (resting) phase at any time. With ~100,000 follicles, this means 10,000–15,000 hairs will shed over the typical 3-month telogen period — approximately 100 per day. Shedding above 150–200 daily, especially with no regrowth, may signal telogen effluvium or another condition worth investigating.

facts.title: Hydration Affects Hair Elasticity

facts.fullDetail: Hair is about 15–35% water by weight. When hydration levels drop, the hair cortex becomes brittle and loses flexibility, making it prone to snapping rather than stretching. Drinking 6–8 glasses of water daily supports not only overall health but specifically the moisture content of the hair shaft and scalp sebum production.

facts.title: The Scalp Has a Unique Microbiome
facts.description: Your scalp hosts a diverse community of bacteria and fungi that, when balanced, protect against dandruff, dermatitis, and infection.

facts.title: Hair Has Three Structural Layers
facts.description: Each hair strand consists of the medulla (core), cortex (middle layer providing strength), and cuticle (outer protective layer of overlapping scales).
facts.fullDetail: The cuticle is the outermost protective layer made of overlapping keratin scales — like roof tiles. When these lie flat, hair appears shiny and smooth. Heat, chemical treatments, and alkaline products raise these scales, causing frizz and breakage. The cortex contains the melanin pigment and determines hair's tensile strength. The medulla is a soft core found mainly in thick hairs.

facts.title: Hair Cannot 'Breathe' — It's Not Alive
facts.description: The hair shaft is composed of dead keratin cells. Only the follicle — below the skin — contains living cells.
facts.fullDetail: This distinction matters enormously. True nourishment must come from within — through blood supply to the follicle. Claims about 'feeding' hair from a shampoo are marketing, not science.

facts.title: Tight Hairstyles Can Cause Permanent Hair Loss

facts.fullDetail: Traction alopecia typically begins as temporary shedding along the hairline. Over years, chronic tension causes follicular scarring and irreversible damage. It disproportionately affects women and people of African descent who frequently wear tight styles. Early intervention — loosening styles and avoiding extensions — can reverse early-stage traction alopecia, but advanced cases are permanent.

facts.title: Iron is Critical for Hair Growth
facts.description: Iron deficiency — even without anaemia — is one of the most common and easily correctable causes of hair shedding, particularly in women.
facts.fullDetail: Ferritin (stored iron) levels below 30 ng/mL are associated with increased telogen shedding. The hair follicle matrix has some of the body's highest cell turnover rates and is acutely sensitive to iron availability. Women of reproductive age are at highest risk due to menstrual iron losses. A simple blood ferritin test can identify this reversible cause of hair loss.

facts.title: Hair Growth Rate Slows With Age
facts.description: Hair growth rate, density, and diameter all decline progressively from around age 30 onwards due to reduced follicle activity and hormonal changes.
facts.fullDetail: The anagen (growth) phase shortens with age — from an average of 4–6 years in youth to 2–3 years by later life — meaning hair never achieves the same maximum length. Follicle miniaturisation, reduced scalp blood flow, and declining growth hormone contribute to this change. This is entirely normal and distinct from pathological hair loss conditions.

facts.title: Heat Styling Raises the Hair Cuticle
facts.description: Excessive heat from straighteners and curling irons raises and cracks the cuticle scales, leading to frizz, dullness, and breakage over time.
facts.fullDetail: Hair protein begins to denature above 150°C (302°F). Most styling tools reach 180–230°C. This disrupts the hydrogen bonds and disulphide bridges in the cortex. Heat protectant sprays form a film over the cuticle and partially absorb thermal stress, but cannot eliminate damage at very high temperatures. Using the lowest effective temperature and applying heat protectant significantly extends hair health.

facts.title: Blood Flow Feeds Your Follicles
facts.description: Hair follicles receive oxygen and nutrients through a dense capillary network in the scalp. Poor circulation limits follicle function.
facts.fullDetail: Each hair follicle sits just above a capillary loop that delivers oxygen, amino acids, vitamins, and hormones directly to the dermal papilla — the command centre of the follicle. facts.title: Genetics Sets the Foundation
facts.description: Your genes determine hair colour, texture, density, and predisposition to androgenetic alopecia (pattern hair loss).
facts.fullDetail: Androgenetic alopecia is polygenic — inherited from both parents, not just the maternal grandfather as the myth suggests. The primary mechanism involves sensitivity of follicles to dihydrotestosterone (DHT), which shortens the anagen phase and miniaturises follicles over successive cycles. Genetic testing can now identify risk variants in genes like AR, HDAC4, and CKMT1 that predict pattern loss risk.

facts.title: Healthy Scalp pH is 4.5–5.5

facts.fullDetail: The acid mantle of the scalp — a thin, protective film — maintains a pH of 4.5–5.5. Alkaline shampoos (pH 7+) strip this mantle, raising the cuticle, causing frizz, and allowing pathogenic microorganisms to colonise the scalp. Look for pH-balanced shampoos (pH 4.5–6.5). Conditioners with acidic pH help reseal the cuticle after washing.

facts.title: Dandruff is Accelerated Scalp Cell Turnover
facts.description: Dandruff is caused by Malassezia fungus triggering accelerated skin cell shedding on the scalp — producing visible white flakes.

In susceptible individuals, its metabolites (oleic acid from sebum breakdown) penetrate the scalp barrier and trigger inflammation, speeding up keratinocyte turnover from the normal 28 days to just 7 days. Anti-dandruff actives like zinc pyrithione, selenium sulphide, and ketoconazole target the fungus rather than the flakes themselves.

facts.title: Sleep Repairs Hair Follicles
facts.description: Deep sleep is when growth hormone is secreted at its peak, stimulating cell renewal in hair follicles and supporting the anagen phase.
facts.fullDetail: Human growth hormone (HGH) is released in pulses during slow-wave sleep. HGH stimulates IGF-1 in the follicle, promoting matrix cell division and extending the anagen phase. Chronic sleep deprivation elevates cortisol (stress hormone), which directly inhibits follicle growth. 7–9 hours of quality sleep per night is an underrated pillar of hair health.

facts.title: Smoking Starves Your Hair Follicles
facts.description: Smoking constricts scalp blood vessels, reducing oxygen and nutrient delivery to follicles, and introduces toxins that accelerate follicle ageing.

Toxic compounds in cigarette smoke also generate reactive oxygen species (free radicals) that damage follicle DNA and accelerate the miniaturisation process seen in androgenetic alopecia. Smokers statistically experience earlier and more severe hair loss.

facts.title: Myth: Hair Loss is Always Genetic
facts.description: While genetics are the most common cause of hair loss, many cases are caused by reversible factors like nutritional deficiencies, stress, thyroid disorders, and medications.

Gentle detangling from tip to root, using a wide-tooth comb on wet hair, is best practice.

facts.title: Iron Deficiency is a Leading Reversible Cause of Hair Loss
facts.description: Low ferritin (stored iron) levels are among the most commonly identified — and most correctable — causes of diffuse hair shedding.
facts.fullDetail: Ferritin acts as an iron reservoir in the body. Hair follicle matrix cells, which divide rapidly, require iron for DNA synthesis. When ferritin drops below 30 ng/mL, follicles are among the first tissues to be deprived. Restoring ferritin above 70–80 ng/mL through supplementation or dietary changes typically reverses iron-related shedding within 3–6 months.

facts.title: Traction Alopecia Can Become Permanent
facts.description: Chronic tension from tight hairstyles damages follicles progressively. If caught early it's reversible; in advanced stages, scarring makes it permanent.
facts.fullDetail: Early traction alopecia presents as folliculitis (small bumps), then as a retreating hairline with short broken hairs. At this stage, stopping the offending style and using topical minoxidil can reverse damage. However, if tension is maintained for years, the follicle undergoes fibrous replacement — scar tissue that permanently prevents regrowth. Annual trichoscopy can detect early-stage changes before they become irreversible.

facts.title: DHT Miniaturises Hair Follicles
facts.description: Dihydrotestosterone (DHT) — converted from testosterone by the enzyme 5-alpha reductase — binds to receptors in susceptible follicles and progressively shrinks them.
facts.fullDetail: In genetically susceptible individuals, DHT shortens the anagen phase with each successive cycle, producing progressively finer, shorter hairs until the follicle produces only vellus (peach fuzz) or stops entirely. DHT concentration in the scalp, not serum testosterone, determines the degree of follicle miniaturisation. Blocking 5-alpha reductase (with finasteride) or DHT binding (with topical agents) can halt or reverse this process.

facts.title: Postpartum Hair Loss is Temporary
facts.description: Women commonly shed large amounts of hair 2–4 months after childbirth due to the hormonal shift at delivery — but it almost always fully reverses.
facts.fullDetail: During pregnancy, elevated oestrogen prolongs the anagen phase, reducing normal shedding and giving hair an unusually full appearance. After delivery, oestrogen drops sharply, releasing all follicles into telogen simultaneously. The result is diffuse shedding — sometimes alarming in volume — starting 2–4 months postpartum. Normal hair density typically restores within 6–12 months without treatment.

facts.title: Hair Porosity Determines Product Absorption
facts.description: Hair porosity — how readily the cuticle absorbs and retains moisture — dictates which products and techniques work best for your hair type.
facts.fullDetail: Low porosity hair has tightly bound cuticle scales that resist moisture entry. High porosity hair (from chemical/heat damage) has raised or damaged cuticle scales that absorb moisture quickly but lose it just as fast. Low porosity hair benefits from lightweight products and heat to open the cuticle; high porosity hair benefits from protein treatments and sealing oils. Understanding porosity is the foundation of an effective hair care routine.

facts.title: Myth: You Can Repair Split Ends With Products
facts.description: Split ends cannot be repaired — they can only be temporarily masked. The only permanent solution is trimming.
facts.fullDetail: Once the hair shaft splits, the cortex fibres separate irreversibly. Products containing silicones, proteins, or bonding agents can temporarily glue the split together and improve its appearance, but normal daily activity re-splits the bond within hours to days. The split will continue to travel up the shaft if not removed. Regular trims (every 8–12 weeks) prevent splits from worsening and maintain hair length better than attempting to 'heal' them.

facts.title: Hair Transplants Relocate Permanent Follicles
facts.description: Hair transplant surgery (FUT or FUE) moves DHT-resistant follicles from the back of the scalp to thinning areas — and the transplanted hairs retain their resistance.
facts.fullDetail: The 'donor dominance' principle, established by Norman Orentreich in 1959, explains why transplanted hairs survive: follicles from the occipital (back) scalp are genetically programmed to be DHT-resistant and retain this characteristic regardless of where they are transplanted. facts.title: Biotin Deficiency Causes Hair Loss — But Deficiency is Rare
facts.description: Biotin (Vitamin B7) is essential for keratin synthesis, but true deficiency is uncommon, and supplementing without confirmed deficiency has no proven hair benefit.
facts.fullDetail: Biotin deficiency causes brittleness, hair loss, and skin rash. However, dietary biotin is widely available (eggs, nuts, seeds, salmon) and the daily requirement is just 30–35μg. Clinical biotin deficiency is rare except in people with biotinidase deficiency, consuming raw egg whites long-term (avidin blocks absorption), or on specific anticonvulsants. The booming biotin supplement market is largely driven by marketing, not evidence — routine supplementation in non-deficient people produces no measurable hair improvement.

facts.title: The Norwood Scale Classifies Male Hair Loss

facts.fullDetail: Norwood Type I shows minimal recession; Type VII is extensive baldness with only a horseshoe of hair remaining. The scale helps dermatologists set treatment expectations: early-stage (I–III) patients respond best to medical therapy; advanced-stage (V–VII) patients may require surgical intervention. A trichoscopy assessment alongside Norwood staging provides a more complete picture of follicle miniaturisation.

facts.title: Myth: Natural Oils Can Regrow Lost Hair
facts.description: No natural oil has been proven to regrow hair in individuals with androgenetic alopecia. Oils can improve scalp condition and reduce breakage, but do not reverse follicle miniaturisation.

facts.description: Trichoscopy (dermoscopy of the scalp) allows dermatologists to assess follicle miniaturisation, density, and scalp conditions without a biopsy.

facts.title: Autophagy Recycling Supports Hair Follicle Stem Cells
facts.description: Autophagy — the cellular recycling process — is critical for maintaining hair follicle stem cell (HFSC) health and enabling repeated regeneration cycles.
facts.fullDetail: Hair follicle stem cells reside in the 'bulge' region of the follicle and activate at the start of each new anagen phase. Autophagy removes damaged organelles from HFSCs, preventing their premature ageing (senescence). Impaired autophagy in HFSCs leads to premature follicle miniaturisation. Intermittent fasting and caloric restriction, which upregulate autophagy, are being investigated for their potential to enhance HFSC longevity.

facts.title: The Wnt Signalling Pathway Controls Follicle Regeneration
facts.description: Wnt/β-catenin signalling is the master regulatory pathway that activates hair follicle stem cells and initiates the anagen phase of each new growth cycle.
facts.fullDetail: At the start of each anagen phase, Wnt ligands from the dermal papilla activate β-catenin in follicle stem cells, triggering their proliferation and differentiation into the new hair matrix. Blocking Wnt signalling arrests follicle cycling; activating it prematurely shortens the resting phase. Multiple companies are developing topical Wnt pathway activators as next-generation hair loss treatments — several are currently in Phase II clinical trials.

facts.title: Ceramides Seal and Protect the Hair Cuticle
facts.description: Ceramides are lipid molecules in the hair cuticle's cell membrane complex. They maintain cuticle integrity, reduce moisture loss, and protect against chemical damage.
facts.fullDetail: The cell membrane complex (CMC) of the hair cuticle contains ceramides, cholesterol, and fatty acids. Bleaching, colouring, and heat remove these lipids, increasing porosity and fragility. Ceramide-containing hair products (shampoos, conditioners, and masks) can partially replenish these lipids through topical absorption, improving tensile strength and reducing breakage. They do not penetrate as deeply as the cortex but offer significant cuticle-level protection.

facts.title: Myth: Going Grey Early Means You'll Go Bald
facts.description: Premature greying and androgenetic alopecia are driven by entirely different mechanisms and share no causal relationship.
facts.fullDetail: Greying is caused by depletion of melanocyte stem cells in the follicle bulge — a process driven by oxidative stress, genetics, and age. Androgenetic alopecia is driven by DHT sensitivity of follicle matrix cells. The two processes are genetically distinct: the MC1R gene influences greying, while the AR and HDAC4 genes influence androgenetic alopecia. A person can grey early with a full head of hair, or maintain their natural colour while experiencing significant hair loss.

facts.title: Androgenetic Alopecia Affects the Vertex First

This gradient explains the predictable Norwood progression pattern and why the 'safe zone' for donor hair in transplantation is the occipital band. Understanding this distribution allows clinicians to predict progression and target preventive treatment to highest-risk zones.

facts.title: Myth: Sunlight Regrows Hair

The distinction is critical: Vitamin D status (addressable via supplements) vs direct sun exposure to the scalp. Wearing sun protection on the scalp is protective, not harmful to hair growth.

facts.title: The Ludwig Scale Has Three Stages for Female Hair Loss
facts.description: The Ludwig classification (I, II, III) grades female pattern hair loss by crown thinning severity, with I being mild and III being extensive.
facts.fullDetail: Ludwig Stage I shows thinning visible on the crown when the hair is parted; Stage II shows pronounced widening of the central parting with visible scalp; Stage III shows diffuse crown thinning with only a thin frontal hairline preserved. Unlike male AGA, the frontal hairline is maintained in most women with FPHL. Treatment success rates decline significantly from Stage I to Stage III, reinforcing the importance of early detection and intervention.

facts.title: Chemical Hair Processing Weakens Disulphide Bonds
facts.description: Perming, relaxing, and bleaching break the disulphide bonds between cysteine residues in cortical keratin chains, permanently altering the hair's mechanical properties.
facts.fullDetail: Disulphide bonds between adjacent keratin proteins give hair its structural strength and shape memory. Chemical reduction (using ammonium thioglycolate in perms, guanidine in relaxers) breaks these bonds to reshape the hair, while oxidation (using hydrogen peroxide) reforms them in the new configuration. Bleaching uses strong oxidants that break some bonds irreversibly. Each chemical treatment cycle incrementally depletes the hair's strength reserve, increasing susceptibility to breakage.

facts.title: Spironolactone Requires Contraception in Women of Childbearing Age

facts.fullDetail: Spironolactone's anti-androgenic effects can cause feminisation of a male foetus (hypospadias, ambiguous genitalia) if taken during pregnancy. For this reason, most prescribers require confirmation of contraception before prescribing. It is contraindicated in pregnancy entirely. This is an important counselling point that distinguishes spironolactone from other FPHL treatments like minoxidil (which should be stopped 1 month before conception attempts rather than being absolutely contraindicated).

quiz.title: Myth: Natural Oils Can Regrow Lost Hair
quiz.description: No natural oil has been proven to regrow hair in individuals with androgenetic alopecia. Oils can improve scalp condition and reduce breakage, but do not reverse follicle miniaturisation.
quiz.fullDetail: Rosemary oil showed comparable effects to 2% minoxidil in one small 2015 study for androgenetic alopecia, generating significant media interest — but the trial was small (100 participants) and has not been independently replicated. Most carrier and essential oils (coconut, argan, castor) improve hair's surface condition by coating the cuticle and reducing friction, and some have anti-fungal scalp benefits. But none have Level 1 evidence for reversing DHT-driven follicle miniaturisation.

quiz.title: Myth: Hair Loss Treatments Work the Same for Everyone
quiz.description: Hair loss treatments vary widely in effectiveness based on individual genetics, the type and stage of hair loss, and underlying causes.
quiz.fullDetail: A treatment that produces dramatic regrowth in one person may be entirely ineffective in another with the same diagnosis. Responsiveness to minoxidil, for example, is linked to the enzyme sulphotransferase — 'sulphator-positive' individuals respond well; 'sulphator-negative' individuals do not. Genetic panels can now predict minoxidil response before starting treatment. Personalised treatment planning, guided by blood tests, trichoscopy, and potentially genetic data, consistently outperforms one-size-fits-all protocols.

quiz.title: Myth: Going Grey Early Means You'll Go Bald

quiz.fullDetail: Greying is caused by depletion of melanocyte stem cells in the follicle bulge — a process driven by oxidative stress, genetics, and age. Androgenetic alopecia is driven by DHT sensitivity of follicle matrix cells. The two processes are genetically distinct: the MC1R gene influences greying, while the AR and HDAC4 genes influence androgenetic alopecia. A person can grey early with a full head of hair, or maintain their natural colour while experiencing significant hair loss.

facts.title: Oxidative Stress Accelerates Follicle Ageing

Antioxidant enzymes (catalase, superoxide dismutase) decline with age, reducing the follicle's defence capacity. Antioxidant compounds — topical or dietary — are being investigated as anti-ageing hair treatments.

facts.title: Telomere Length Correlates With Hair Follicle Lifespan

Research indicates that androgenetic alopecia follicles show accelerated telomere shortening versus age-matched controls — possibly due to elevated local oxidative stress from DHT metabolism byproducts. Telomerase activation (which lengthens telomeres) in hair follicles is theoretically possible but carries oncogenic risk, making it a complex therapeutic challenge.

facts.title: Laser Hair Removal Permanently Destroys Hair Follicles
facts.description: Laser hair removal uses selective photothermolysis — targeting melanin in the follicle bulb — to permanently damage the follicle and prevent regrowth.
facts.fullDetail: Laser wavelengths (typically 755nm alexandrite, 810nm diode, or 1064nm Nd:YAG) are preferentially absorbed by melanin in the hair bulb and shaft. The absorbed energy converts to heat that destroys the bulb and bulge stem cells. Multiple sessions are required because only anagen hairs are effectively targeted. Paradoxical hypertrichosis (stimulation of vellus hair growth) can occasionally occur around treated areas, particularly at sub-threshold energies.

facts.title: Nutritional Ketosis May Affect Hair Growth Phases
facts.description: Very low-carbohydrate (ketogenic) diets have been associated with increased hair shedding during the adaptation phase — likely due to rapid metabolic change rather than nutrient deficiency.
facts.fullDetail: Several case series report telogen effluvium occurring 2–4 months into strict ketogenic diets. The proposed mechanism is metabolic stress from rapid fuel-source transition reducing IGF-1 and insulin levels, both of which support anagen. Severe caloric restriction accompanying ketogenic eating also depletes biotin, zinc, and iron. Shedding typically resolves within 6 months as the body adapts, and ensuring adequate micronutrient intake reduces the risk.

facts.title: Myth: Biotin Supplements Are Essential for Hair Health
facts.description: Biotin supplements are widely sold for hair health but have no proven benefit in individuals without biotin deficiency — which is rare in developed countries.
facts.fullDetail: A 2017 systematic review found that all published cases of biotin supplementation improving hair condition occurred in patients with confirmed biotin deficiency or rare inherited biotinidase disorders. In non-deficient individuals, supplementation adds no measurable benefit and may cause elevated biotin levels that interfere with immunoassay-based medical tests (including thyroid function and cardiac troponin), potentially causing dangerous diagnostic errors. Look for actual deficiency evidence before supplementing.

quiz.title: Myth: Coloured Hair Falls Out More
quiz.description: Hair dye does not cause follicle damage or increase hair shedding. It affects the shaft, not the follicle below the skin.
quiz.fullDetail: Chemical hair dye lifts the cuticle scales using alkaline agents (ammonia or ethanolamine) and oxidises natural melanin with hydrogen peroxide. This can increase shaft porosity and mechanical fragility — making coloured hair more prone to breakage. But breakage is not follicle-level hair loss, and colouring does not affect the cycling of follicles. The shedding some people notice after colouring is typically telogen hairs that were already in the pipeline.

There is no mechanism by which water, shampoo, or massage causes hairs to exit the anagen phase prematurely.

facts.title: Oral Minoxidil at Low Dose Has Superior Efficacy
facts.description: Oral minoxidil 0.25–2.5mg daily achieves higher plasma concentrations than topical use, with multiple studies showing greater hair regrowth with fewer tolerability issues at low doses.
facts.fullDetail: Topical minoxidil requires the enzyme sulphotransferase to convert it to its active form in the scalp — and ~30–40% of people lack adequate enzyme activity ('non-responders'). Oral administration bypasses this limitation, delivering pre-formed active minoxidil systemically. A 2022 meta-analysis found oral minoxidil produced significantly greater terminal hair count increases than topical. Common side effects include hypertrichosis (body hair growth) and mild fluid retention. It is typically prescribed off-label.

facts.title: BMP Signalling Maintains Follicle Stem Cell Quiescence
facts.description: Bone morphogenetic protein (BMP) signalling keeps hair follicle stem cells in quiescence during telogen and must be overcome to initiate anagen.
facts.fullDetail: BMP signals from the dermal papilla and arrector pili muscle act as a 'brake' on follicle stem cell activation. Wnt and BMP inhibitors (Noggin, BAMBI) released at the beginning of anagen overcome this inhibition, allowing stem cells to proliferate. The balance of BMP vs Wnt-activating signals determines the timing of anagen onset. Understanding this balance could allow pharmacological shortening of the telogen phase as a hair loss treatment.

facts.title: Reactive Oxygen Species Impair DHT Detoxification in Follicles
facts.description: Oxidative stress products reduce the activity of enzymes that metabolise DHT within the follicle, amplifying DHT's miniaturisation effect.
facts.fullDetail: Cytochrome P450 enzymes in follicle cells normally metabolise and inactivate DHT. However, ROS generated by inflammation (from seborrhoeic dermatitis, UV damage, or smoking) inhibit these enzymes, allowing DHT to accumulate to higher local concentrations. This creates a vicious cycle: inflammation raises ROS, ROS impairs DHT clearance, and higher DHT drives more inflammation. Anti-inflammatory scalp treatment in AGA patients may therefore have an indirect DHT-lowering effect.

facts.title: Hairline Design in Transplantation Requires Artistic Precision
facts.description: Natural-looking hair transplant results depend as much on artistic hairline design as technical follicle implantation — age-appropriate, asymmetric design is critical.
facts.fullDetail: The ideal transplanted hairline should be naturally irregular (not geometrically straight), slightly asymmetric, and designed to be appropriate for the patient's projected long-term hair loss trajectory. Placing hair too low in a young patient risks an unnatural appearance as native hair continues to recede around the transplanted zone. Single follicle grafts should be used in the hairline, with multi-follicle grafts behind, mirroring natural density distribution.

facts.title: Fibrosis Around Follicles Predicts Treatment Response
facts.description: Perifollicular fibrosis — scar tissue forming around follicles — detected via trichoscopy or biopsy reduces the likelihood of response to medical hair loss treatment.
facts.fullDetail: In androgenetic alopecia, chronic inflammation and DHT-mediated follicle stress can trigger the accumulation of fibrous tissue around follicle sheaths. Trichoscopy signs include peripilar casts (scaling around hair shafts) and perifollicular grey-white discolouration. Histologically confirmed perifollicular fibrosis correlates with poorer response to minoxidil. Anti-fibrotic treatments (topical retinoids, corticosteroids) may improve the follicular microenvironment before starting growth-promoting therapy.

facts.title: Chronic Alcohol Consumption Depletes Hair Nutrients
facts.description: Regular heavy alcohol use depletes zinc, biotin, B vitamins, and protein — all essential for healthy hair growth — while disrupting hormonal balance.
facts.fullDetail: Alcohol impairs absorption of zinc (via competition with mucosal transporters), biotin (via gut dysbiosis), and B12 (via reduced intrinsic factor production). It increases oestrogen and reduces testosterone, affecting DHT dynamics. Alcohol-related liver damage impairs protein metabolism, reducing amino acid availability for keratin synthesis. Regular heavy drinking is a recognised but underappreciated contributing factor to hair loss that often remains unaddressed in clinical consultations.

facts.description: The trichogram involves plucking 40–60 hairs and counting anagen vs telogen roots under a microscope to measure the 'anagen:telogen ratio' as a quantitative hair health indicator.
facts.fullDetail: Normally, 85–90% of hairs are in anagen at any time. A trichogram ratio below 80% anagen indicates a pathologically elevated telogen rate, consistent with telogen effluvium. This technique is simple, inexpensive, and can be performed in outpatient settings. Modern phototrichograms use serial photography rather than hair plucking, avoiding the trauma and discomfort of the original method while providing comparable data.

A small number of patients report persistent symptoms after discontinuation — termed Post-Finasteride Syndrome (PFS) — but controlled studies have not consistently confirmed causation vs coincidence. However, alopecia areata can affect any scalp region including the back; scarring alopecias can spread from the crown backwards; traction alopecia from low buns or dreadlocks preferentially affects the nape. facts.title: Post-COVID Hair Loss is Telogen Effluvium — Not Permanent
facts.description: Hair loss reported by millions after COVID-19 infection is a classic post-illness telogen effluvium, almost universally temporary and reversing within 6–12 months.
facts.fullDetail: COVID-19 triggers telogen effluvium through multiple mechanisms: fever (direct follicle shock), systemic inflammation (elevated IL-6, TNF-α), physiological stress, nutritional depletion (especially zinc and Vitamin D), and psychological stress. The shedding typically begins 2–3 months after infection and peaks at 3–4 months. Population studies confirm almost universal recovery within 12 months without specific intervention, though nutritional correction may accelerate recovery.

In some scarring alopecias, destruction of the arrector pili muscle precedes follicle loss, suggesting it plays a protective role in follicle integrity.

facts.title: In Vitro Fertilisation Hormones Can Trigger Shedding
facts.description: The supraphysiological oestrogen levels used in IVF stimulation protocols, followed by their rapid decline, can trigger telogen effluvium 2–4 months after treatment.
facts.fullDetail: IVF ovarian stimulation uses gonadotrophins to elevate oestrogen to 5–10x normal levels. This artificially prolongs anagen in a similar fashion to pregnancy. When stimulation ends and oestrogen drops, a synchronised telogen entry can follow. Additionally, the psychological and physiological stress of IVF cycles can amplify the shedding response. As with postpartum hair loss, recovery is expected within 6–12 months and rarely requires specific hair loss treatment.

Has no hormonal effect on hair

facts.title: Myth: Only Dermatologists Can Treat Hair Loss
facts.description: Many GPs, trichologists, and specialist nurses are now trained to diagnose and manage androgenetic alopecia and common reversible hair loss conditions.
facts.fullDetail: Blood test protocols for common hair loss causes, standardised trichoscopy techniques, and evidence-based prescribing guidelines mean that AGA and telogen effluvium can be competently managed in primary care. Scarring alopecias, treatment-refractory alopecia areata, and complex multi-factorial cases warrant dermatology referral. Trichologists can provide valuable diagnostic input for many cosmetic and maintenance aspects of hair care.
facts.mythStatement: Only specialist dermatologists can diagnose and treat hair loss

facts.title: Myth: Thinning Hair Means Baldness is Inevitable
facts.description: Diffuse thinning has many reversible causes, and even androgenetic alopecia can be indefinitely stabilised with appropriate treatment.
facts.fullDetail: Telogen effluvium from nutritional deficiency, stress, illness, or hormonal change causes dramatic thinning that fully reverses once the cause is addressed. Even in AGA — where genetic progression is real — early intervention with medical treatment can maintain density for decades. Trichoscopy-guided treatment monitoring allows timely adjustment of regimens to stay ahead of progression.
facts.mythStatement: Experiencing hair thinning means baldness is inevitable

facts.title: Myth: Hair Transplants Look Obviously Artificial
facts.description: Modern FUE hair transplants performed by skilled surgeons are virtually undetectable, even under close inspection.
facts.fullDetail: The 'pluggy' appearance of historic hair transplants used large round grafts of 10–20 hairs. Modern FUE uses individual follicular units of 1–3 hairs placed at the correct angle, direction, and density to mirror natural growth patterns. Poor results today are almost always due to inadequate design, wrong density planning, or surgeon inexperience.
facts.mythStatement: Hair transplants always look obviously artificial

facts.title: Myth: Stress Causes Permanent Baldness
facts.description: Stress-induced telogen effluvium is temporary and fully reversible — stress does not permanently destroy follicles.
facts.fullDetail: Telogen effluvium triggered by stress pushes follicles prematurely into the resting phase, not into permanent inactivity. Once the stressor resolves and cortisol normalises, affected follicles re-enter anagen. Only if AGA is simultaneously present will stress appear to accelerate permanent loss by unmasking underlying genetic progression.
facts.mythStatement: Stress causes permanent hair loss by destroying follicles

quizOptions.text: ✅ TRUE — this is accurate
❌ FALSE — this is a myth

facts.title: Myth: Hair Loss Medication Must Be Taken Forever
facts.description: For AGA, indefinite treatment is typically required to maintain gains — stopping medication leads to resumed progression within months.
facts.fullDetail: For androgenetic alopecia, the underlying DHT sensitivity and genetic follicle vulnerability persists regardless of treatment. Cessation of finasteride or minoxidil leads to resumption of progression within 3–12 months, with most gained hair lost within 1–2 years. By contrast, resolved telogen effluvium does not require long-term treatment, and alopecia areata may spontaneously remit in ~50% of moderate cases.
facts.mythStatement: You can stop hair loss medication once your hair has regrown

quiz.description: Scalp punch biopsies allow precise measurement of local DHT concentration and androgen receptor density, guiding personalised treatment intensity.
quiz.fullDetail: Hair follicle androgen sensitivity varies dramatically across the scalp — even adjacent follicles can have different receptor densities. A 3mm punch biopsy sent for immunohistochemistry can quantify AR expression and provide a 'miniaturisation index'. Combined with trichoscopy, this offers the most precise diagnostic picture available, enabling clinicians to target high-density treatment to highest-risk zones.

quiz.title: Fibroblast Growth Factor 7 (FGF7) Targets Follicle Regeneration
quiz.description: FGF7 (keratinocyte growth factor) stimulates hair matrix cell proliferation and extends anagen — it is a potential topical treatment target under investigation.
quiz.fullDetail: FGF7 is produced by dermal papilla cells and signals via FGFR2b on hair matrix keratinocytes. In mouse models, FGF7 overexpression dramatically prolongs anagen and increases hair density. Recombinant FGF7 topical formulations are in preclinical development. Understanding FGF signalling cascades in the follicle is helping researchers design more precise anagen-extending therapeutics beyond minoxidil's broad vasoactive mechanism.

facts.title: Topical Tretinoin Enhances Minoxidil Penetration
facts.description: Tretinoin (retinoic acid) at 0.01–0.025% applied with minoxidil increases follicular absorption by up to 2–3×, improving treatment efficacy.
facts.fullDetail: Retinoic acid upregulates the expression of prostaglandin endoperoxide synthase and enhances the conversion of minoxidil sulphate in dermal papilla cells. Studies show that combination minoxidil + tretinoin outperforms minoxidil alone in vertex hair density at 24 weeks. The concentration must be kept low to avoid retinoid dermatitis — higher concentrations paradoxically impair the barrier and reduce absorption.

facts.title: Wnt/β-Catenin Pathway Activators Are in Phase II Clinical Trials
facts.description: Small-molecule Wnt pathway activators (e.g. SM04554) have entered Phase II trials for androgenetic alopecia, targeting follicle regeneration at a molecular level.
facts.fullDetail: The Wnt/β-catenin signalling cascade governs hair follicle induction during embryogenesis and is required for anagen re-entry in adult follicles. Inhibition by Dkk-1 (dickkopf-1) in the balding scalp suppresses Wnt signalling and contributes to miniaturisation. Topical SM04554 increased terminal hair counts by ~10% over placebo in Phase I/II data — modest but mechanistically significant as a first-in-class regenerative approach.

facts.title: Androgenetic Alopecia Has Over 250 Genetic Risk Loci
facts.description: GWAS studies have identified over 250 genetic variants associated with AGA risk, most on autosomal chromosomes — not just the androgen receptor gene on the X chromosome.
facts.fullDetail: Early models of AGA genetics overemphasised maternal inheritance via the X-linked AR gene. Large-scale genome-wide association studies now show AGA is highly polygenic, with risk loci on chromosomes 1, 2, 7, 17, and 20 among others. Variants near genes involved in hair follicle cycling, immune function, and WNT signalling all contribute. Clinical genetic testing for AGA risk remains premature given the complexity of the polygenic architecture.

Only inherited from the mother

facts.title: Hair Shaft Cosmetics Work at the Surface Level Only
facts.description: Cosmetic products — including oils, serums, and bond-builders — cannot alter the follicle or affect new growth; they only modify the existing hair shaft.
facts.fullDetail: The hair shaft above the scalp surface is biochemically inert dead tissue. No topically applied ingredient can penetrate to the cortex without breaking disulphide bonds (as in chemical processing). Bond-building treatments (Olaplex technology) work on disulphide bonds in chemically damaged hair but have no follicular effect. Marketing claims of 'follicle nourishment' from shampoos are not supported by evidence — the active follicle is insulated from the scalp surface by 3–4mm of tissue.

They work only on the existing hair shaft surface
They penetrate deep into the dermis
They alter the hair's DNA structure

facts.title: Myth: Hair Loss is More Distressing for Women Than Men
facts.description: Hair loss causes significant psychological distress in both sexes — clinical studies show that men report equal or greater emotional impact when assessed with standardised scales.
facts.fullDetail: Societal narratives assume women suffer more from hair loss, but evidence-based studies using the Dermatology Life Quality Index and Hair-Specific Quality of Life tools show men also report high distress, depression, and reduced self-esteem. Men are less likely to seek help or disclose distress due to stigma. The assumption that male hair loss is cosmetically acceptable while female hair loss is devastating reinforces harmful gender norms and delays men accessing care.
facts.mythStatement: Hair loss distress is primarily a female problem

facts.title: JAK1/2 Inhibitors Show Durable Response in Severe Alopecia Areata
facts.description: Oral baricitinib and ritlecitinib (JAK inhibitors) are now FDA-approved for severe alopecia areata, with response rates of 30–40% at 36 weeks.
facts.fullDetail: Alopecia areata is driven by collapse of immune privilege around the hair follicle, with CD8+ T-cells attacking follicle antigens. JAK inhibitors block the JAK-STAT signalling cascade used by inflammatory cytokines (IFN-γ, IL-15) to recruit and activate these T-cells. Baricitinib was the first systemic treatment FDA-approved specifically for severe AA in 2022. Ritlecitinib followed in 2023. Response requires ongoing treatment; discontinuation leads to relapse in most patients.

quizOptions.text: They permanently cure alopecia areata
They achieve response rates of 30–40% at 36 weeks
They only work for androgenetic alopecia
They require topical application only

facts.title: Clascoterone (Breezula) Targets Androgen Receptors Directly in the Scalp
facts.description: Clascoterone is a topical androgen receptor antagonist that competitively blocks DHT at the follicle level without systemic anti-androgen effects.
facts.fullDetail: Unlike oral finasteride (which reduces systemic DHT production) or oral spironolactone (systemic anti-androgen), clascoterone acts locally at the scalp androgen receptor. Phase III trials show significant hair count improvement vs placebo in both men and women, with a favourable systemic safety profile — no measurable effect on serum testosterone. This makes it a candidate for men concerned about finasteride's systemic side effects and for women as an alternative to spironolactone.

facts.title: Follicle Neogenesis Occurs After Deep Wounding in Adult Skin
facts.description: Contrary to the long-held belief that adult mammals cannot generate new follicles, studies show that deep skin wounding activates embryonic morphogenesis pathways and can produce new follicles.
facts.fullDetail: Research by Cotsarelis et al. demonstrated that large excisional wounds in mice trigger a WNT-driven de novo follicle formation, termed 'wound-induced hair neogenesis' (WIHN). The newly formed follicles originate from interfollicular epidermal cells rather than surviving follicle stem cells. Early human studies suggest limited WIHN is possible in adults, opening a theoretical avenue for follicle regeneration therapy — though translation to clinical hair restoration remains years away.

Deep wounding can activate WNT-driven de novo follicle formation
Only stem cell injections can create new follicles

facts.title: Hydrogen Peroxide Accumulation Drives Age-Related Hair Greying
facts.description: As follicles age, catalase activity declines and H₂O₂ accumulates to millimolar concentrations, bleaching melanin from the inside — this is the primary cause of greying.
facts.fullDetail: Melanocyte stem cells in the follicle bulge replenish pigment-producing melanocytes each hair cycle. With ageing, oxidative stress from H₂O₂ accumulation damages melanocyte stem cell DNA and causes their premature differentiation or apoptosis. Reduced catalase (the enzyme that breaks down H₂O₂) and reduced methionine sulfoxide reductase activity amplify this effect. Some researchers have explored pseudocatalase-based topical preparations, though efficacy in reversing grey hair in humans remains unproven.

quizOptions.text: Melanocytes simply stop producing melanin randomly
H₂O₂ accumulation bleaches melanin due to declining catalase activity
UV light is the primary cause of greying

facts.title: The Hair Follicle is an Immune-Privileged Site That Can Collapse
facts.description: Hair follicles suppress local immune activity to protect melanocyte and follicle antigens — collapse of this privilege is the core pathomechanism of alopecia areata.
facts.fullDetail: The follicle immune privilege (FIP) is maintained by low MHC class I expression, local TGF-β1, α-MSH, and ACTH secretion. These factors prevent cytotoxic T-cells from recognising follicle antigens. Collapse of FIP — triggered by IFN-γ upregulation and NKG2D ligand expression — allows CD8+ NKG2D+ T-cells to attack the follicle, causing the arrested anagen and acute hair shedding characteristic of AA. Understanding FIP collapse directly led to the development of JAK inhibitors as AA treatments.

Collapse of follicle immune privilege allows T-cells to attack the follicle
Follicles have no interaction with the immune system
AA is purely a genetic condition with no immune component

facts.title: Myth: PRP Always Works for Hair Loss
facts.description: PRP efficacy varies widely depending on preparation method, platelet concentration, activation technique, and patient selection — it is not universally effective.
facts.fullDetail: Platelet-rich plasma studies show highly variable results because there is no standardised PRP protocol. Platelet concentration, presence of leukocytes, use of activators (CaCl₂ vs thrombin), injection depth, and frequency all affect outcomes. Meta-analyses show moderate overall benefit for AGA, but individual responses range from excellent to no effect. AGA patients with more follicle miniaturisation tend to respond better than those with completely scarred follicles. PRP is best viewed as an adjunct, not a standalone curative treatment.
facts.mythStatement: PRP treatment always regrows hair effectively for all patients

facts.title: Sonic Hedgehog (SHH) Signalling Controls Hair Follicle Morphogenesis
facts.description: The Sonic Hedgehog pathway is essential for hair follicle development during embryogenesis and for regulating the bulge stem cell niche in adult follicles.
facts.fullDetail: SHH secreted by the follicle matrix activates Gli transcription factors in surrounding dermal cells, driving follicle downgrowth and differentiation during development. In adult follicles, SHH signalling is reactivated at anagen onset. Pharmacological SHH activation has been explored as a hair growth stimulant, though concerns about oncogenic potential (SHH pathway mutations cause basal cell carcinoma) have limited clinical translation. Understanding SHH's role contextualises the follicle as a model system for studying stem cell niche biology.

SHH has no role in adult hair follicle biology

facts.title: Exosome Therapy Delivers Growth Factor Cargo Directly to Follicles
facts.description: Stem cell-derived exosomes contain microRNAs, growth factors (VEGF, FGF-7, IGF-1), and signalling molecules that promote anagen and reduce follicle inflammation when injected intradermally.
facts.fullDetail: Exosomes are nano-sized extracellular vesicles (30–150nm) secreted by cells as intercellular communication messengers. Dermal exosome preparations from adipose or Wharton's jelly-derived mesenchymal stem cells have shown hair count improvements in pilot clinical studies. Proposed mechanisms include promoting follicle stem cell activation, increasing dermal papilla cell proliferation, and suppressing the microinflammation associated with AGA. Regulatory approval is pending; quality control of exosome preparations is a significant manufacturing challenge.

They block DHT at the scalp surface

facts.title: Myth: Hair Cloning is Available Now
facts.description: Hair follicle cloning — multiplying donor follicles in vitro for unlimited transplantation — remains in experimental stages and is not yet clinically available.
facts.fullDetail: Hair cloning (or follicle cell multiplication) aims to solve the donor hair scarcity problem in transplant surgery by growing dermal papilla cells in culture and using them to induce new follicle formation. The challenge is that human dermal papilla cells rapidly lose their hair-inductive capacity in 2D culture. 3D spheroid culture methods partially preserve inductive properties, and companies like Stemson Therapeutics and HairClone are developing protocols. Commercial availability is projected no sooner than the early 2030s, pending safety and efficacy trials.
facts.mythStatement: Hair follicle cloning technology is already available for patients
